Your jetski has these key watercraft characteristics:

1. **Steering depends on jet thrust.**  
Jet thrust turns the watercraft. If you completely release the throttle, only minimum thrust is produced. At speeds above trolling speed, your ability to steer will quickly decrease without throttle. You may still be able to turn briefly right after releasing the throttle, but once the engine slows down, the watercraft will not respond to handlebar input until you apply throttle again or slow to trolling speed.

2. **Practice turning in open water.**  
Practice turning in an open area without obstructions until you become familiar with this handling characteristic.

3. **Remove debris only with the engine shut off.**  
Stop the engine and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch before removing any debris or weeds that may have collected around the jet intake.

<PIC>

4. **It is water-jet propelled and has no neutral.**  
This watercraft is powered by a water jet pump directly connected to the engine. That means whenever the engine is running, some thrust is produced and the watercraft may move. There is no “neutral” position.

5. **Keep clear of the intake grate and jet nozzle.**  
Keep away from the intake grate while the engine is on. Long hair, loose clothing, or PFD straps can get caught in moving parts, which could result in severe injury or drowning.

6. **Never put anything into the jet thrust nozzle while the engine is running.**  
Contact with the rotating parts of the jet pump could cause severe injury or death.

Your jetski is legally considered a powerboat, so you must obey the navigation rules of the waterway whenever you meet another vessel.

Here are the main rules to follow:

Stand-on vessel: If you have the right-of-way, maintain your course and speed, except when necessary to avoid an immediate collision.

Give-way vessel: If you do not have the right-of-way, take early, positive, and clear action to stay out of the way. Normally, do not cross in front of the stand-on vessel. Slow down or change direction briefly and pass behind it so your intentions are obvious.

If a collision appears unavoidable, neither boat has the right-of-way. Both boats must take action to avoid the collision.

<PIC>

Meeting: If you are approaching another power-driven vessel head-on and there is a risk of collision, neither vessel has the right-of-way. Both should alter course to avoid each other. In practice, both vessels should turn to starboard (right), so you pass with the other vessel on your port (left) side.

<PIC>

Crossing: When two power-driven vessels are crossing and there is a risk of collision, the vessel that has the other vessel on its starboard (right) side must keep out of the way. If the other vessel is on your starboard side, you are the give-way vessel. If the other vessel is on your port (left) side, you are normally the stand-on vessel and should maintain course and speed, as long as the other vessel gives way.

<PIC>

Overtaking: If you are passing another vessel, you are the give-way vessel and must stay clear until you have completely passed and are safely clear. If another vessel is overtaking you, maintain your speed and direction so it can pass safely.

Also remember:
1. In narrow channels, keep to the right when safe and practical.
2. Fishing vessels using nets, lines, or trawls generally have the right-of-way, but they should not impede other vessels in narrow channels.
3. Sailing vessels should normally be given the right-of-way, except in special cases such as when the sailboat is overtaking, when it must keep clear of a fishing vessel, or when it is in a narrow channel.
4. Maintain a safe distance from other boats, watch for ski ropes and fishing lines, and check behind you before turning.
5. Operate defensively at a safe speed. A jetski does not have brakes, and steering ability is greatly reduced if you fully release the throttle, so take action early to avoid collisions.,['Manual20_26', 'Manual20_27', 'Manual20_28']

The Quick Shift Trim System (QSTS) selector is located at the left handlebar grip and is used to adjust the trim angle of the watercraft.

Operating the QSTS selector changes the angle of the jet thrust nozzle vertically, which changes the trim angle of the watercraft.

There are 5 positions on the QSTS selector:
- A and B: bow down
- N: neutral
- C and D: bow up

To change the trim angle:
1. Reduce engine speed to 3,000 r/min or less.
2. Squeeze the shift lock lever, then turn the QSTS selector to the desired position.
3. Release the shift lock lever to lock the QSTS selector in place.

<PIC>

CAUTION:
Do not turn the QSTS selector while operating the watercraft at full throttle, otherwise damage could occur to the QSTS.

The neutral “N” position provides good performance for most operating conditions. If needed, you can select bow down or bow up to suit the riding condition or performance you want.

<PIC>

Bow up (C or D) raises the bow while the watercraft is on plane. This puts less of the bow in the water, reduces water resistance, and can improve straight-ahead acceleration on plane and top speed. However, in some conditions the watercraft may porpoise (hop in the water). If that happens, select neutral or bow down.

Bow down (A or B) lowers the bow more into the water.

<PIC>,['Manual20_51', 'Manual20_52', 'Manual20_53']

The fuel meter and hour meter are both part of the multifunction information center and help you monitor your WaveRunner while riding.

<PIC>

**Fuel meter**: The fuel meter lets you check the fuel level while riding. It uses **eight segments** to show the approximate amount of fuel remaining in the tank. Because the reading can vary depending on operating conditions, it should be used as a **reference** rather than an exact measurement. When fuel drops to about **13 L (3.4 US gal, 2.9 Imp gal)**, the lowest two segments begin blinking as a low-fuel warning.

<PIC>

**Hour meter**: The hour meter shows the **total engine operating hours** since the watercraft was new. Its main purpose is to help you **follow the maintenance schedule** and track service intervals.

<PIC>,['Manual20_54', 'Manual20_58', 'Manual20_59']

To inspect the fuel filter and fuel tank on your 2005 WaveRunner, follow the guidance below:

**Fuel filter**

This watercraft uses a **one-piece, disposable fuel filter**.

- Inspect the filter area for signs of **water in the filter**.
- The fuel filter should be **replaced after the initial 10 hours or first month of operation**, and then **every 200 hours or 24 months**, or **any time water is found in the filter**.
- If replacement is needed, **have a Yamaha dealer perform the work**.

<PIC>

**Important safety warning:**
Do **not** try to replace the fuel filter yourself. If the filter is installed incorrectly, it can leak gasoline, which may cause a **fire or explosion**. If service is needed, have a **Yamaha dealer** replace it.

**Fuel tank**

Inspect the fuel tank for:
- **fuel leakage**
- **water in the tank**

If you find water in the fuel system, or if the fuel tank needs cleaning, have a **Yamaha dealer service the watercraft**.

If the intake or impeller is clogged with weeds or debris, clean it as follows:

If weeds or debris get caught in the intake or impeller, cavitation can occur. This can reduce jet thrust even though engine speed increases, and if it continues, the engine may overheat and seize. If you suspect a blockage, beach the watercraft and inspect the intake and impeller. Always stop the engine before beaching the watercraft.

<PIC>

Before removing any weeds or debris from the jet intake or impeller area, shut the engine off and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch. Contact with the rotating parts of the jet pump can cause severe injury or death.

1. Turn the watercraft onto its port (left) side as shown.

CAUTION:
- Place a clean cloth or carpet under the watercraft to help prevent scratches and abrasions.
- Always turn the watercraft onto its port (left) side only.
- Support the bow when turning it on its side so the handlebars are not bent or damaged.

<PIC>

2. Remove any weeds or debris from around the drive shaft, impeller, pump housing, and jet thrust nozzle.

If the debris is difficult to remove, consult your Yamaha dealer.

To help prevent this issue, avoid operating in areas with heavy weed growth when possible. If you must ride through weedy areas, alternate between partial throttle and full throttle, since weeds tend to build up more at steady speed or trolling speed.,['Manual20_85', 'Manual20_86']

Here’s how to start your WaveRunner in different situations.

First, for normal starting:
1. Attach the engine shut-off cord (lanyard) to your left wrist.
2. Install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
3. Push the start switch (green button) to start the engine.

The engine will not start if the clip is not installed.

<PIC>

<PIC>

Before starting off, always scan constantly for people, objects, swimmers, and other watercraft. Operate defensively, keep a safe speed, and maintain a safe distance.

<PIC>

Starting and boarding in shallow water:
1. Do not start the engine in less than 60 cm (2 ft) of water. Move the watercraft to water deep enough, then board from the side or rear.
<PIC>
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
3. Grip the handlebars with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and start off.

Starting from a dock:
1. Board the watercraft from the side.
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
3. Push the watercraft away from the dock, grip the handlebars with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and start off.

<PIC>

Boarding alone:
1. Swim to the rear of the watercraft, place both hands on the boarding platform, pull yourself up, and grasp the handgrip.
<PIC>
2. Pull yourself up to a kneeling position, then move onto the seat and sit astride.
<PIC>
3. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
4. Grip the handlebars firmly with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and look in all directions before starting off.
<PIC>

Boarding with a passenger:
1. The operator boards first, sits astride the seat, attaches the engine shut-off cord to the wrist, and installs the clip.
2. Have the passenger get on board and sit on the seat.
<PIC>
3. Make sure the passenger’s feet are on the floor of the footwell and that the passenger is securely holding on to the operator.
4. Start the engine, look in all directions, and then accelerate to planing speed.
<PIC>

If it is difficult to balance with a passenger:
1. The passenger should steady the watercraft while the operator boards and gets into position.
<PIC>
2. Attach the lanyard to your left wrist, install the clip, start the engine, and keep it at idle.
3. The passenger pulls up onto the platform, kneels, and then crawls onto the seat as the watercraft accelerates.
<PIC>
4. Accelerate to planing speed, then reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.
<PIC>

Important safety notes:
- Do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated, has both feet on the footwell floor, and is holding on securely.
- This watercraft has no neutral position, so some movement can occur whenever the engine is running.
- Do not release the throttle when trying to steer away from an object, because throttle is needed for steering.,['Manual20_44', 'Manual20_48', 'Manual20_87', 'Manual20_88', 'Manual20_89', 'Manual20_71', 'Manual20_72', 'Manual20_73', 'Manual20_90', 'Manual20_92', 'jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

Basic steps for boarding your jetski with a passenger in deep water:

1. Board the watercraft first using the same method as boarding alone, then sit astride the seat. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.

2. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft.

3. Have the passenger climb on from the rear and sit on the seat.

<PIC>

When the passenger is getting on board, both the operator and the passenger should work together to keep the watercraft balanced.

4. The operator should confirm that the passenger has both feet on the floor of the footwell and is securely holding on to the operator.

5. Start the engine, check carefully in all directions, and then accelerate to planing speed.

<PIC>

Before starting off, make sure there are no boats, obstructions, or swimmers around you.

<PIC>

Warning: Do not apply throttle until the passenger is fully seated, has both feet on the floor of the footwell, and is holding on to the operator.

Note: The heavier the combined weight of the operator, passenger, and cargo, the harder the watercraft will be to balance. Do not exceed the maximum load of 160 kg (353 lb).,['Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'Manual20_92']

If it is difficult to balance the watercraft at a standstill, proceed as follows:

1. The passenger must steady the watercraft while the operator is boarding. The operator pulls himself up onto the platform into a seating position, then balances the watercraft.
<PIC>

2. Attach the lanyard to your left wrist, then install the clip on the lanyard to the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle.

3. The passenger pulls himself up on the platform, kneels down, then crawls onto the seat as the watercraft accelerates.
<PIC>

4. The watercraft can now be accelerated to planing speed, then the operator can reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.

To load the lawn mower onto a trailer or truck safely:

Use a heavy-duty trailer or truck with a full-width ramp. Do not use separate narrow ramps for each side of the machine. Make sure the ramp angle does not exceed 15 degrees, and the ramp length is at least 4 times the height of the trailer or truck bed from the ground.

<PIC>

Loading steps:
1. If you are using a trailer, attach it securely to the towing vehicle and connect the safety chains.
2. If applicable, connect the trailer brakes and lights.
3. Lower the ramp and make sure the angle between the ramp and the ground is no more than 15 degrees.
4. Back the machine up the ramp onto the trailer.

<PIC>

After loading:
5. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and engage the parking brake.
6. Secure the machine near the front caster wheels and the rear bumper using straps, chains, cables, or ropes, following local tie-down requirements.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:
- Use extreme caution when operating the machine on the ramp.
- Avoid sudden acceleration, braking, or direction changes on the ramp, as this could cause loss of control or tip-over.
- When unloading, lower the ramp the same way and drive the machine forward down the ramp.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64', 'Manual23_65']

To unload the lawn mower safely:

1. Lower the ramp, and make sure the ramp angle does not exceed 15 degrees.

<PIC>

2. Drive the machine forward down the ramp.

<PIC>

Important:
- Use only a full-width ramp.
- Use extreme caution when operating on the ramp.
- Avoid sudden acceleration or deceleration, as this could cause loss of control or tip-over.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64']

To change the engine oil of the lawn mower:

1. Start the engine and let it run for 5 minutes.
   This warms the oil so it drains more easily.

2. Park the machine so that the drain side is slightly lower than the opposite side.
   This helps the oil drain completely.

3. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO) and engage the parking brake.

4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.

5. Drain the oil from the engine:
   - Pull the drain hose out from the frame.
   - Place the end of the hose into a drain pan.
   - Use 2 wrenches to loosen and remove the drain plug.
   - Allow the oil to drain fully.
   - Reinstall the plug and tighten it securely.

<PIC>

6. Refill the engine oil:
   - Slowly pour about 80% of the specified oil into the filler tube.
   - Wait about 3 minutes for the oil to settle.
   - Insert the dipstick to check the level.
   - Do not screw the dipstick/cap in when checking the level.
   - Add oil gradually until the level reaches the Full mark.
   - Reinstall and tighten the dipstick/cap.

<PIC>

7. Start the engine and drive to a flat area.

8. Shut off the engine and check the oil level again.
   Add oil if needed to bring it to the Full mark.

Use detergent engine oil as specified for your engine:
- Kawasaki engines: API service SF, SG, SH, SJ, or SL
- Kohler engines: API service SJ or higher

Service interval:
- Change the engine oil every 100 hours, or more often in dirty or dusty conditions.

Important: Dispose of the used oil at a recycling center.,['Manual23_75', 'Manual23_76']

To remove the air-cleaner filters on a lawn mower, follow the steps for your engine type below.

For Kawasaki engines:
1. Park the machine on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), and engage the parking brake.
2. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.
3. Release the latches on the air cleaner and pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.
<PIC>

5. Gently slide the primary filter out of the air-cleaner body. Avoid knocking the filter against the side of the body.
6. Remove the safety filter only if you are replacing it.

For Kohler engines:
1. Park the machine on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), and engage the parking brake.
2. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.
3. Release the latches on the air cleaner and pull the air-inlet cover off the air-cleaner body.
4. Clean the air-inlet screen and cover.
5. Install the air-inlet cover and secure it with the latches.
<PIC>
6. Release the latches on the air cleaner and pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.
7. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
8. Gently slide the primary filter out of the air-cleaner body. Avoid knocking the filter against the side of the body.
<PIC>
9. Remove the inner filter only if you intend to replace it.,['Manual23_72', 'Manual23_82', 'Manual23_83']

10. Reinstall the belt guide on the idler arm.
11. Using the 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole, reinstall the idler spring.

You can configure the motherboard’s PCI Express 3.0 x16 slots in the BIOS.

Go to:
**Advanced > System Agent (SA) Configuration > PEG Port Configuration**

Then set:
- **PCIEx16_1 Link Speed**
- **PCIEx16_2 Link Speed**

Available options are:
**[Auto] [Gen1] [Gen2] [Gen3]**

The actual operating mode of the PCIe 3.0 x16 slots depends on how many VGA/PCIe cards are installed:

<PIC>

- **Single VGA/PCIe card:** **PCIe 3.0 x16_1** (gray) runs at **x16** and is the recommended slot for the best performance.
- **Dual VGA/PCIe cards:** **PCIe 3.0 x16_1** and **PCIe 3.0 x16_2** run at **x8 / x8**.
- **Triple VGA/PCIe cards:** **PCIe 3.0 x16_1** and **PCIe 3.0 x16_2** run at **x8 / x8**, and **PCIe 3.0 x16_3** runs at **x4**.

So, in BIOS you can set the **link speed** for the main PCIe x16 slots, while the **lane operating mode** (x16, x8/x8, or x8/x8/x4) changes automatically based on the card configuration.,['Manual25_31']

Before changing any motherboard jumper setting, turn OFF the computer and unplug the power cord.

On this motherboard, different jumpers are set in different ways:
- **CLRTC (2-pin)**: clear CMOS/RTC RAM by temporarily shorting the two pins.
- **CPU_OV (3-pin)**: move the jumper cap between different pin pairs to change the setting.

For **Clear RTC RAM (2-pin CLRTC)**:
<PIC>
To erase the RTC RAM:
1. Turn OFF the computer and unplug the power cord.
2. Use a metal object such as a screwdriver to short the two pins.
3. Plug the power cord and turn ON the computer.
4. Hold down the <Del> key during the boot process and enter BIOS setup to reenter data.

If the steps above do not help, remove the onboard battery and short the two pins again to clear the CMOS RTC RAM data. After clearing the CMOS, reinstall the battery.

You do not need to clear the RTC when the system hangs due to overclocking. For system failure caused by overclocking, shut down and reboot the system so the BIOS can automatically reset parameters to their default values through the CPU Parameter Recall (C.P.R.) feature.

For **CPU Over Voltage jumper (3-pin CPU_OV)**:
<PIC>
The CPU Over Voltage jumper allows you to set a higher CPU voltage for a flexible overclocking system, depending on the type of the installed CPU.
- To gain more CPU voltage setting, insert the jumper cap on **pins 2-3**.
- To go back to the default CPU voltage setting, insert the jumper cap on **pins 1-2**.

In short, to set motherboard jumpers, always power off first, then either **short the specified pins** or **place the jumper cap on the correct pin pair**, according to the label and function of the jumper.,['Manual25_33', 'Manual25_34']

You can identify and connect the motherboard rear panel connectors as follows:

<PIC>

1. **PS/2 Mouse/Keyboard combo port**: Connects to a PS/2 mouse or PS/2 keyboard.  
2. **DisplayPort**: Connects to DisplayPort-compatible display devices.  
3. **VGA port**: Connects to a VGA monitor or other VGA-compatible devices.  
4. **LAN (RJ-45) port**: Connects the motherboard to a Local Area Network (LAN).

<PIC>

**LAN port LED indications:**
- **Activity/Link LED**  
  - Off: No link  
  - Orange: Linked  
  - Orange blinking: Data activity  
  - Orange blinking, then steady: Ready to wake up from S5 mode  
- **Speed LED**  
  - Off: 10 Mbps connection  
  - Orange: 100 Mbps connection  
  - Green: 1 Gbps connection

5. **Center/Subwoofer port (orange)**: Connects the center/subwoofer speakers.  
6. **Rear Speaker Out port (black)**: Connects rear speakers in 4.1 / 5.1 / 7.1-channel mode.  
7. **Line In port (light blue)**: Connects audio sources such as CD/DVD players.  
8. **Line Out port (lime)**: Connects headphones or speakers; in multi-channel mode, this becomes **Front Speaker Out**.  
9. **Microphone port (pink)**: Connects to a microphone.  
10. **Optical S/PDIF out port**: Connects to amplified speakers, headphones, or S/PDIF-compliant devices.

**Audio port use by channel mode:**
- **2-channel**: Light Blue = Line In, Lime = Line Out, Pink = Mic In  
- **4.1-channel**: Black = Rear Speaker Out  
- **5.1-channel**: Orange = Center/Subwoofer, Black = Rear Speaker Out  
- **7.1-channel**: Orange = Center/Subwoofer, Black = Rear Speaker Out, Optical S/PDIF = Side Speaker Out

<PIC>

11. **USB 3.1 Type-A port**: For USB 3.1 devices.  
12. **USB 3.1 Type-C port**: For USB 3.1 mobile or peripheral devices.  
13. **USB 3.0 ports 3–6**: For USB 3.0 devices. For best performance, connect USB 3.0 devices to these ports.  
14. **DVI-D port**: Connects to DVI-D compatible display devices. Note: DVI-D cannot be converted to RGB output for CRT and is not compatible with DVI-I.  
15. **HDMI port**: Connects to HDMI display devices and supports HDCP-protected content playback.  
16. **USB 2.0 ports 13 and 14**: For USB 2.0/1.1 devices.

**Important notes:**
- The rear display ports available are **DisplayPort, VGA, DVI-D, and HDMI**.  
- Multi-display output supports up to **three displays in Windows**, **two in BIOS**, and **one in DOS**.  
- USB 3.0 devices may run in xHCI or EHCI mode depending on the operating system settings.  
- For faster and better performance, it is recommended to use the USB 3.0/3.1 ports for compatible devices.,['Manual25_35', 'Manual25_36', 'Manual25_37', 'Manual25_38']

Save a copy of the original motherboard BIOS file to a USB flash disk in case you need to restore the BIOS in the future.

You can update the motherboard BIOS file using one of these methods:

1. EZ Update
EZ Update is a utility that allows you to automatically update your motherboard’s softwares, drivers and the BIOS version easily. With this utility, you can also manually update the saved BIOS and select a boot logo when the system goes into POST.

To launch EZ Update, click EZ Update on the AI Suite 3 main menu bar.

<PIC>

EZ Update requires an Internet connection either through a network or an ISP.

2. EZ Flash 3
The EZ Flash 3 feature allows you to update the BIOS without using an OS-based utility.

<PIC>

To update the BIOS using EZ Flash 3:
1. Enter the Advanced Mode of the BIOS setup program. Go to the Tool menu to select EZ Flash 3 Utility and press Enter.
2. Update the BIOS via USB or Internet.

Via USB:
a) Insert the USB flash disk that contains the latest BIOS file to the USB port, then select by USB.
b) Press Tab to switch to the Drive field.
c) Use the Up/Down arrow keys to find the USB flash disk, then press Enter.
d) Press Tab to switch to the Folder Info field.
e) Use the Up/Down arrow keys to find the BIOS file, then press Enter to perform the BIOS update.

Via the Internet:
a) Select by Internet.
b) Use the Left/Right arrow keys to select an Internet connection method, then press Enter.
c) Follow the onscreen instructions to complete the update.

3. Reboot the system when the update process is done.

Note: EZ Flash 3 supports USB devices with FAT32/16 format and single partition only. Do not shut down or reset the system while updating the BIOS.

3. BIOS Updater in DOS environment
BIOS Updater allows you to update the BIOS in DOS environment.

Before updating BIOS:
• Prepare the motherboard support DVD and a USB flash drive.
• Download the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater and save them in your USB flash drive.
• Ensure that your USB flash drive is in single partition and in FAT32/16 format.

To update the BIOS file:
1. On the FreeDOS prompt, type bupdater /pc /g and press Enter.
2. On the BIOS Updater screen, press Tab to switch from Files panel to Drives panel then select D:.

<PIC>

3. Press Tab to switch to the Files panel, select the BIOS file, and press Enter.
4. After the BIOS Updater checks the selected BIOS file, select Yes to confirm the BIOS update.

<PIC>

5. Select Yes then press Enter. When BIOS update is done, press ESC to exit BIOS Updater.
6. Restart your computer.

Do not shut down or reset the system while updating the BIOS to prevent system boot failure.,['Manual25_60', 'Manual25_61', 'Manual25_63', 'Manual25_64']

To prepare for installing the CPU on the motherboard, follow these precautions first:

<PIC>

- Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any component.
- Before handling components, use a grounded wrist strap or touch a safely grounded metal object, such as the power supply case, to prevent static damage.
- Hold components by the edges, and avoid touching the ICs.
- If you remove any component, place it on a grounded anti-static pad or in the anti-static bag that came with it.
- Before installing or removing any component, make sure the ATX power supply is switched off or the power cord is disconnected from the power supply.

This motherboard uses a surface-mount LGA1151 socket designed for 6th Generation processors.

<PIC>

CPU socket: LGA1151

Before CPU installation:
- Unplug all power cables.
- Make sure you are using a CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket only. Do not install CPUs made for LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 sockets.
- Check that the PnP cap is installed on the socket and that the socket contacts are not bent.
- If the PnP cap is missing or you notice damage to the cap, socket contacts, or motherboard components, contact your retailer immediately.

The sealing ring is a key part of the **pressure cooking lid** on the multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. When the pressure cooking lid is closed, it creates an **air-tight seal** between the lid and the inner pot so the cooker can build and maintain pressure.

The sealing ring **must be installed before use**, and **only one sealing ring should be fitted in the lid at a time**.

Tip: Because silicone is porous, it can absorb strong smells and flavours. Keeping extra sealing rings can help reduce flavour transfer between different dishes.

<PIC>

**Important safety information**  
Only use **authorized** sealing rings made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. **Do not use** a sealing ring that is stretched, deformed, cut, or otherwise damaged.

Before cooking, always check that the sealing ring:
- is installed correctly
- has no cuts
- is not warped or stretched

With normal use, sealing rings naturally stretch over time. They should generally be **replaced every 12–18 months**, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, or damage.

Failure to follow these instructions may cause food to discharge, which could result in personal injury or property damage.

**To remove the sealing ring:**  
Grip the edge of the silicone ring and pull it out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.

<PIC>

**To install the sealing ring:**  
Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack and press it firmly into place all the way around to prevent puckering.

When installed correctly, the sealing ring should sit snugly behind the sealing ring rack and **should not fall out when the lid is turned over**.

<PIC>,['Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

Here’s what you should know about the **steam release valve** on the multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer:

**1. Location**  
It is located on the **top of the pressure cooking lid**, near the **float valve** and **quick release button**.

<PIC>

**2. What it does**  
The steam release valve **sits loosely on the steam release pipe**. During pressure release, **steam is expelled from the top of the steam release valve**. This is normal and is an important part of how the cooker safely releases pressure. The steam release valve is **essential for safe pressure cooking**.

<PIC>

**3. Important safety information**  
- **Do not cover or block** the steam release valve in any way.  
- **Do not place your hands, face, or exposed skin over it** while the appliance is operating or while there is still residual pressure.  
- Before use, it should be checked for **clogging** as part of normal safety inspection.

**4. Cleaning and maintenance**  
You can remove and reinstall the steam release valve for cleaning:

**Remove the steam release valve:**  
Pull the steam release valve **straight up** and off the steam release pipe. The steam release valve **must be installed before using the cooker**.

<PIC>

**Install the steam release valve:**  
Place the steam release valve onto the steam release pipe and **press down firmly**. When installed correctly, it will still **sit loosely**, but it should **stay in place when the lid is turned over**.

<PIC>

If steam is escaping from the steam release valve in a **steady stream for longer than 3 minutes** when it should not be, the appliance should be turned off and allowed to depressurize before opening.,['Manual30_3', 'Manual30_15', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button is located on the top of the pressure cooking lid, near the steam release valve and float valve.

<PIC>

It controls the steam release valve, which determines whether the cooker stays sealed or releases pressure.

<PIC>

It has 2 positions:
- **Seal position**: the button is popped up. Use this position to keep pressure inside the cooker.
- **Vent position**: the button is pressed down. Use this position to release pressure.

**What’s helpful about it:**
- It is easy to operate.
- It helps keep your hands farther away from the steam during pressure release.

**Important to know:**
- The lid may say **Turn to Seal**, but the motion is more like a small flick.
- Do **not** turn the quick release button more than **45°**.
- After moving it, let it spring back to its original position.

For a **Quick Release**, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the **Vent** position.

<PIC>

When you do this, steam will jet out from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal. Once all pressure has been released, the **float valve drops down into the lid**.

<PIC>

**Safety notes:**
- Do not place your hands, face, or exposed skin over the steam release valve while releasing pressure.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve.
- Do not remove the lid while the float valve is still up.
- Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods, or foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains.,['Manual30_3', 'Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release (NR or NPR) is one of the pressure release methods used after pressure cooking on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.

With Natural Release, the cooker is not vented manually. Instead, as the temperature inside drops, the pressure decreases gradually on its own.

<PIC>

What to do:
After cooking is complete, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position and turn Keep Warm off.

What to expect:
Your food will continue cooking for a while from residual heat. This is often called “carryover cooking” or “resting,” and it can be especially useful for some foods.

The time needed for natural depressurization depends on the amount, type, and temperature of the food and liquid inside the pot. In some cases, it can take up to 40 minutes.

When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop back down into the lid. Only then is it safe to open the lid.

Natural Release is recommended for high-starch foods such as soups, stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal, and congee, as well as foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains.

Important safety note:
Do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is still up, and never force the lid open.,['Manual30_9']

   - Fully open: for cold engine starting
   - Half-open: for warming up the engine
   - Close: for warm engine starting

3. Pull the recoil starter slowly until it engages, then pull it briskly.
4. After the engine starts, move the starter lever (choke) to the “Half-open” position.
5. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

<PIC>

Electric starting model:

2. Turn the main switch to the “START” position.
3. After the engine starts, move the starter lever (choke) to the “Half-open” position.
4. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

<PIC>

- Release the switch immediately after the engine starts.

Before starting the engine, check throttle, brake, and steering for proper operation.

<PIC>

When riding, the operator should always keep both hands on the handlebars. To move and steer, press the throttle lever slowly to move the machine, then turn the handlebar in the desired direction.

For turning, slow down as you approach a curve and begin to turn the handlebars in the desired direction. Put your weight on the running board to the inside of the turn and lean your upper body into the turn.

<PIC>

Practice this procedure at slow speed many times in a large flat area with no obstacles. Avoid higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with the operation of your snowmobile.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_127']

The correct way to turn a snowmobile on most snow surfaces is to use “body English,” meaning you help the turn by shifting your body weight correctly.

1. As you approach a curve, slow down.
2. Begin turning the handlebars in the direction you want to go.
3. At the same time, put your weight on the running board on the inside of the turn.
4. Lean your upper body into the turn.
5. The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you need to lean.

This technique should first be practiced many times at low speed in a large, flat area with no obstacles. After you are comfortable with it, you can use it in tighter curves or at somewhat higher speeds.

Be aware that improper riding procedures, such as abrupt throttle changes, excessive braking, incorrect body movements, or carrying too much speed into a turn, may cause the snowmobile to tip.

If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean farther into the turn to regain balance. If needed, gradually ease off the throttle and/or steer toward the outside of the turn.

<PIC>

Remember: Avoid higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with the operation of your snowmobile.,['Manual34_127']

Proper uphill snowmobile riding techniques are:

1. Start by practicing on gentle slopes. Only attempt steeper or more difficult climbs after you have built enough skill and confidence.

2. As you approach the hill, gain some speed before beginning the climb. Once you are on the hill, reduce the throttle opening as needed to help prevent track slippage.

3. Keep your body weight on the uphill side at all times. When climbing straight up the hill, lean forward. On steeper slopes, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars.

<PIC>

4. Slow down as you near the crest of the hill. Be ready for obstacles, sudden drop-offs, or other people or vehicles that may be on the other side.

5. If you cannot continue climbing, do not spin the track. Stop the engine and set the parking brake. Then pull the rear of the snowmobile around so the machine points back downhill. Do not stand on the downhill side of the machine. Once it is facing downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and carefully descend.

Warning: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginners or novice snowmobilers.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill, keep your speed to a minimum. Apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged while descending. This helps the engine compression slow the machine and prevents the snowmobile from free-rolling down the hill. Also use the brake frequently with light pressure.

<PIC>

WARNING: Use extra caution when braking during descent. Excessive braking can lock the track, causing a loss of control.,['Manual34_128']

Side hills are not recommended for a beginner or novice snowmobiler.

Crossing the face of a slope (side hill) requires you to properly position your weight to maintain proper balance. As you travel across the slope, lean your body to position your weight towards the uphill side. A recommended riding position is to kneel with the knee of the downhill leg on the seat and the foot of the uphill leg on the running board. This position will make it easier for you to shift your body weight as needed.

<PIC>

Snow and ice are slippery, so be prepared for the possibility that your snowmobile could begin to slip sideways on the slope. If this happens, steer in the direction of the slide if there are no obstacles in your path. As you regain proper balance, gradually steer again in the direction you wish to travel.

If your snowmobile starts to tip, steer down the hill to regain balance.

If you are unable to maintain correct balance, and your snowmobile is going to tip over, dismount your snowmobile immediately on the uphill side.,['Manual34_129']

To inspect the spark plug on a snowmobile, follow these steps:

1. Let the engine cool first. The engine and muffler can be very hot after running, so avoid touching them until they have cooled.

2. Remove the spark plug periodically and inspect it. Heat and deposits will cause any spark plug to slowly break down and erode over time.

3. Check the color of the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode. The ideal color is medium to light tan for a normally ridden machine. If the spark plug shows a distinctly different color, there may be an engine problem. In that case, have the machine checked by your dealer.

4. Make sure the spark plug has the correct thread length, or reach. If the reach is too long, overheating and engine damage may result. If the reach is too short, spark plug fouling, poor performance, carbon buildup on exposed threads, hot spots, and thread damage may result.

   - BR250T: 12.7 mm (0.50 in)
   - CS340E / ET410TR / VK540E: 19.0 mm (0.75 in)

<PIC>

5. Before installing the spark plug, measure the electrode gap with a wire thickness gauge and adjust it to specification.

<PIC>

6. When reinstalling the plug, clean the gasket surface, wipe any grime from the threads, and tighten the spark plug properly.

Spark plug torque: 28 Nm (2.8 m-kg, 20 ft-lb),['Manual34_138', 'Manual34_139']

To clean a snowmobile:

1. Thoroughly clean the machine, inside and out, to remove any corrosive salts and acids that may accumulate.
2. Use Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent product, to loosen mud, grease, and grime.
3. Wash the machine with mild soap.
4. Rinse it completely.
5. Dry it completely.

If the snowmobile has been transported through areas where road salt is used, clean it as soon as you arrive at your destination to remove corrosive salt and help prevent corrosion.

There are two ways to memorize channels using **MANUAL PROGRAM**:

## 1. Using the MEMORY/ERASE button
This is the quickest method if your remote has a **MEMORY/ERASE** button.

1. Use the **▲/▼** buttons or the **NUMBER** buttons to select the channel you want to add or remove.
2. Press the **MEMORY/ERASE** button.
   - Select **Memory** to store the channel.
   - Select **Erase** to remove the channel.

<PIC>

## 2. Using the MENU
Use this method if you want to manually set channel memory through the on-screen menu.

1. Press the **MENU** button until the **CH.** menu is displayed.
2. Press **▶**, then use **▲/▼** to select **Manual**.
3. Press **▶**, then use **▲/▼** to select **Channel**.
4. Press **▶**, then use **▲/▼** to select the channel number you want to memorize or erase.

<PIC>

5. Press **▶**, then use **▲/▼** to select **Memory**.

<PIC>

6. Press **▶**, then use **▲/▼** to choose:
   - **On** = memorize/store the channel
   - **Off** = erase/remove the channel
7. Press the **MENU** button repeatedly to exit.

These are the two recommended manual methods for memorizing communication channels on this TV.,['Manual35_9', 'Manual35_10', 'Manual35_11']

Common causes and conditions that can lead to poor television or radio reception include:

1. Ignition or electrical interference: The picture or signal may flutter, drift, or suffer from black spots, noise, or horizontal streaking. This is usually caused by interference from automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, and other electrical appliances.
<PIC>

2. Ghosting: This happens when the TV signal travels along more than one path before reaching the receiver. One path is direct, while another is reflected by tall buildings, hills, or other large objects. This can create double or blurred images. Changing the antenna’s position or direction may help.
<PIC>

3. Snow or weak-signal noise: If the receiver is in a weak or fringe signal area, the picture may be covered with small dots or noise. In these cases, reception may improve with a better or special antenna.
<PIC>

Other possible causes include:
- Strong random interference from cars or airplanes.
- A weak signal coming from the antenna.
- Poor antenna connections, damaged antenna cable, or a badly positioned antenna.
- Local interference from nearby electrical sources.
- Station or channel trouble.

In short, poor reception is commonly caused by interference, reflected signals, weak broadcast strength, antenna problems, or issues at the broadcasting station.,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03']

To enable and customize captions and on-screen text settings on the television, you can use either the **CAPTION** button on the remote or the **MENU** settings.

<PIC>

**Method 1: Using the CAPTION button**
1. Press the **CAPTION** button on the remote.
2. Press the **CAPTION** button repeatedly to cycle through:
   - **Off**
   - **Mode1**
   - **Mode2**
   - **Text1**
   - **Text2**
3. Press the **ENTER (O)** button to exit.

<PIC>

**Method 2: Using the MENU button**
1. Press the **MENU** button.
2. Use the **up/down** buttons to select the **Special** menu.
3. Press the **right** button, then use the **up/down** buttons to select **Caption**.
4. Press the **right** button again, then use the **up/down** buttons to choose:
   - **Off**
   - **Mode1**
   - **Mode2**
   - **Text1**
   - **Text2**
5. Repeatedly press the **MENU** button to exit.

<PIC>

**What the options mean**
- **Mode1**: Usually displays standard closed captions.
- **Mode2**: May display alternate caption information, such as demonstration or programming information.
- **Text1 / Text2**: Show full-screen text services when available, such as program listings, weather, stock information, or news.

**Note:**
- This feature is available on **some models only**.
- The TV remembers the last caption/text mode selected, even after the TV is turned off.,['Manual35_0', 'Manual35_37', 'Manual35_38']

For the best reception, use an outdoor antenna and inspect the antenna and cable first. Severely weathered antennas or cables can reduce signal quality.

<PIC>

How to connect the outdoor antenna:

1. If you are using 300 ohm flat wire:
   - Attach the 300 ohm flat wire to the screws on the 300 to 75 ohm adapter.
   - Tighten the connections securely.
   - Insert the adapter into the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

2. If you are using a 75 ohm coaxial cable:
   - Connect the 75 ohm coaxial cable directly to the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

3. If you are using a CATV cable:
   - Connect the 75 ohm CATV cable directly to the 75 ohm antenna jack.

After the antenna is connected, program the channels:

1. Press the AUTO PRG. button on the remote control.
2. Press the AUTO PRG. button again, or press the indicated start button on screen, to begin AUTO PROGRAM.
3. The TV will search for and memorize all active channels in your area.
4. If needed, you can stop auto programming by pressing ENTER; only the channels found up to that point will be saved.

<PIC>

If the reception is still poor after setup, adjust the antenna direction or position. This can help improve signal problems such as ghosting or weak reception.

<PIC>

In weak signal areas, a snowy picture may mean a stronger or special antenna is needed.

<PIC>,['Manual35_39', 'Manual35_5', 'television0_02', 'television0_03']

To properly connect a DVD player to this TV for the best supported picture and sound, follow these steps:

1. Connect the DVD player’s component video outputs to the TV’s COMPONENT DVD INPUT (480i):
   - DVD Y → TV Y
   - DVD Pb/PB → TV PB
   - DVD Pr/PR → TV PR

2. Connect the DVD player’s audio outputs to the TV’s audio input ports:
   - DVD Audio L → TV Audio L
   - DVD Audio R → TV Audio R

<PIC>

Note: Some DVD players use different labels for component video outputs. Match them like this:
- Y → Y
- Cb / B-Y / Pb / PB → PB
- Cr / R-Y / Pr / PR → PR

<PIC>

3. Turn on the TV.

4. Press the TV/VIDEO button on the remote control, or the TV/VIDEO button on the TV front panel, and select COMPONENT.

5. Turn on the DVD player, then start playback.

For optimal performance on this model, use the component video connection shown above and make sure the cables are connected to the matching Y/PB/PR and L/R audio ports.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44']

We understand that encountering technical issues can be very frustrating and we are committed to helping you resolve any malfunctions as quickly as possible. To effectively troubleshoot the situation, please first verify the power source and ensure the device is properly connected or fully charged. You should then check all cables and physical connections for any loose parts or visible damage that might disrupt performance. It is also essential to confirm that your device software or firmware is updated to the latest version and to try a standard system restart or factory reset to clear any temporary glitches. Additionally, please review the user manual for specific error code meanings and inspect the environment for factors like overheating or signal interference. If these steps do not resolve the issue, please provide your order information and a detailed description of the problem so we can offer further professional technical support or arrange for an appropriate after-sales solution.

To ensure safe operation during this pressure cooking process, follow these precautions at each stage:

<PIC>

**Pre-heating**
- During pre-heating, the cooker vaporizes liquid to create steam.
- Once enough steam builds up, the float valve pops up and locks the lid in place.
- Be aware that frozen foods take longer to pre-heat, and larger amounts of food and liquid increase the time needed to pressurize.

<PIC>

**Cooking**
- Cooking begins only after the cooker reaches the required pressure level.
- During cooking, the cooker automatically maintains pressure at **High** or **Low** pressure.
- A higher pressure means a higher temperature.

<PIC>

**Depressurization**
- Even after cooking is finished, the cooker is still **hot and pressurized**.
- Follow your recipe instructions to depressurize the cooker before opening it.
- The lid can only be removed safely after the pressure has been reduced.

**DANGER:** Do **not** attempt to remove the lid while the float valve is up, and **never** force the lid open. The contents are under extreme pressure. The float valve **must be down** before you try to remove the lid.

**WARNING:** Steam released from the steam release valve is **hot**. Do **not** cover the steam release valve, and do **not** place your hands, face, or exposed skin over it while releasing pressure.

**NOTICE:** Do **not** use **Quick Release (QR)** for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods such as stews, chilis, pasta, and congee, or for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains.,['Manual30_6', 'Manual30_7', 'Manual30_8']

Your power toothbrush has 3 intensity settings:

- High intensity: three lights
- Medium intensity: two lights
- Low intensity: one light

<PIC>

To adjust the intensity to suit your oral care needs, press the intensity indicator lights on the handle to cycle through the options until you reach your preferred setting. The intensity can be changed before, during, or after brushing.

- Choose low intensity if you prefer a gentler brushing experience.
- Choose medium intensity for a balanced setting.
- Choose high intensity if you prefer a stronger brushing feel.

<PIC>

Note: The intensity setting can also be customized from the app. The app lets you customize toothbrush settings according to your preferred modes, intensity and toothbrush feedback.,['toothbrush0_06', 'toothbrush0_07']

You can activate or deactivate the following toothbrush features for customized use:
- Adaptive Intensity
- Pressure Sensor Feedback
- Scrubbing Feedback
- Brush head replacement reminder

Note: If Pressure Sensor Feedback is deactivated, Adaptive Intensity will also be deactivated.

There are 2 ways to manage these features:

From the app
You can use the app to activate or deactivate:
- Adaptive Intensity
- Scrubbing Feedback

First connect the toothbrush to the app, then go to the toothbrush settings in the app to change these features.

From the handle
You can use the handle to activate or deactivate:
- Adaptive Intensity
- Pressure Sensor Feedback
- Brush head replacement reminder

Step 1: Place the handle on the charging stand.

Step 2: Press and hold the power button.

<PIC>

Use the hold time below for the feature you want to change:
- Adaptive Intensity: hold for up to 3 seconds. You will hear 1 beep, and the SenseIQ indicator lights up for 3 seconds.
- Pressure Sensor Feedback: hold for up to 5 seconds. You will hear 1 beep and then 2 beeps, and the SenseIQ indicator and light ring light up purple for 3 seconds.
- Brush head replacement reminder: hold for up to 7 seconds. You will hear 1 beep, then 2 beeps, and then 3 beeps, and the brush head replacement reminder indicator lights up for 3 seconds.

<PIC>

Confirmation of the setting change:
- Activated: the battery indicator blinks white 3 times and you hear 3 tones from low to high.
- Deactivated: the battery indicator blinks amber 3 times and you hear 3 tones from high to low.,['Manual37_13', 'toothbrush0_11']

3. Place the toothbrush into the travel case.
